perturbation.original_text: The federal and state courts within the State of California shall have excl usive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to resolve any disputes arising out of this agreement through binding arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause specifying California courts as having exclusive jurisdiction and replacing it with mandatory binding arbitration can violate California law regarding the enforceability of arbitration agreements, especially if the agreement is deemed unconscionable or unduly favors one party.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1281.2 outlines the court's role in ordering arbitration unless grounds exist to revoke the agreement. By removing the explicit jurisdiction of California courts, the contract could be interpreted in a way that bypasses judicial review of the arbitration agreement's fairness, potentially violating California's requirements for a valid and enforceable arbitration clause, particularly concerning unconscionability and fairness.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.2. On petition of a party to an arbitration agreement alleging the existence of a written agreement to arbitrate a controversy and that a party to the agreement refuses to arbitrate that controversy, the court shall order the petitioner and the respondent to arbitrate the controversy if it determines that an agreement to arbitrate the controversy exists, unless it determines that: (a) The right to compel arbitration has been waived by the petitioner; or (b) Grounds exist for rescission of the agreement. (c) A party to the arbitration agreement is also a party to a pending court action or special proceeding with a third party, arising out of the same transaction or series of related transactions and there is a possibility of conflicting rulings on a common issue of law or fact. For purposes of this section, a pending court action or special proceeding includes an action or proceeding initiated by the party refusing to arbitrate after the petit
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies California courts as having exclusive jurisdiction, while the changed text mandates binding arbitration, removing the court's role. This omission creates a legal contradiction because it bypasses judicial review, potentially violating California's requirements for a valid arbitration clause, especially regarding fairness. CCP 1281.2 allows a court to order arbitration if a written agreement exists, unless grounds for rescission exist. By removing the court's explicit jurisdiction, the contract avoids a court's determination of whether grounds for rescission exist, thus contradicting the legal procedure outlined in CCP 1281.2.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the intern al laws of the State of California, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in The United States of America and worldwide.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with principles of equity and fairness.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific governing law (California) and replacing it with a vague reference to 'principles of equity and fairness' creates ambiguity and removes the protections afforded by California law. This can disadvantage consumers who rely on the predictability and established legal framework of California contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed. By omitting the specific reference to California law, the agreement fails to establish a clear legal framework for interpretation, creating a potential contradiction if the 'principles of equity and fairness' are interpreted in a way that conflicts with established California legal precedent, thus impacting consumer rights and protections under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies California law as the governing law, providing a clear legal framework. The changed text replaces this with 'principles of equity and fairness,' which is vague and lacks a defined legal basis. This omission contradicts California Civil Code § 1646, as detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that a contract should be interpreted according to the law of the place of performance or where it is made. By removing the reference to California law, the changed text creates ambiguity and undermines the legally mandated framework for interpreting the contract, thereby creating a legal contradiction, as it omits a legally mandated framework for consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that its obliga tions hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the Discloser and its business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate the Discloser for any breach of any covenants and agreements set forth herein. A ccordingly, Recipient agrees an d acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, th e Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the necessity of proving actual damages.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that any violation or threatened violation will cause injury to the Discloser and that the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "without the necessity of proving actual damages" from the injunctive relief clause could potentially violate California law, which generally requires a showing of actual harm or a likelihood of harm for injunctive relief to be granted, especially in commercial disputes.  The original text attempts to waive this requirement, which may be unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 526 outlines situations where an injunction may or may not be granted. Although it doesn't explicitly forbid waiving the need to prove actual damages in all cases, California courts often require a showing of irreparable harm for injunctive relief, especially in contract disputes. Removing the language about not needing to prove actual damages might be interpreted as an unenforceable waiver of a procedural requirement, and the courts may still require such a showing, creating a contradiction with the intent of the modified contract.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 526. (a) An injunction may be granted in the following cases: (1) When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a limited period or perpetually. (2) When it appears by the complaint or affidavits that the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action. (3) When it appears, during the litigation, that a party to the action is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party to the action respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. (4) When pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. (5) Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. (6) Where the
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly waives the necessity of proving actual damages for injunctive relief. The modified text removes this waiver. According to the Law Explanation, California courts often require a showing of irreparable harm for injunctive relief, especially in contract disputes. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 526(a)(2), mentions 'great or irreparable injury' as a condition for granting an injunction. By removing the waiver, the modified contract contradicts the original intent of avoiding the need to prove such injury, potentially making it more difficult for the Discloser to obtain injunctive relief as initially agreed.

perturbation.original_text: Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be promptly returned, accompanied by copies of such documentation, after the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.changed_text: Any materials or documents furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be returned after the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase ", accompanied by copies of such documentation," could violate trade secret laws in California. If the 'materials or documents' contain trade secrets, and the recipient is not required to return *all* copies or provide copies that are returned, it weakens the trade secret protection, especially if those copies remain with the recipient. This removal facilitates a potential violation of California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1 allows for injunctive relief against misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the requirement to return all copies, the contract weakens the Discloser's ability to prevent future misappropriation. If the Recipient retains copies, it increases the risk of unauthorized use or disclosure, potentially undermining the protections offered by the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act. While the NDA addresses confidentiality, this omission directly impacts the mandatory return of materials containing trade secrets, potentially contradicting the intent and effect of the CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The omission of the phrase ", accompanied by copies of such documentation," from the original text creates a legal contradiction. The original contract ensures that all materials, including copies, are returned to the Discloser after evaluation. Removing this requirement weakens the Discloser's ability to protect its trade secrets, potentially violating California Civil Code § 3426.1, as the Recipient could retain copies and increase the risk of unauthorized use or disclosure. The law snippet defines 'Misappropriation' which can occur if trade secrets are disclosed or used without consent, which is more likely if copies are not returned as the original text stipulated. By removing the obligation to return copies, the changed text undermines the Discloser's right to prevent misappropriation as per California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
